mcqs.question: What is the index of the first element in a zero-based array?
mcqs.explanation: In zero-based indexing (used in C, C++, Java, Python), the first element is accessed using index 0. This is because the index represents the offset from the base address.

mcqs.question: Which operation has O(1) time complexity in an array?
mcqs.explanation: Accessing an element by its index is O(1) because arrays provide direct memory address calculation: address = base + (index × element_size).

mcqs.question: What type of memory allocation do arrays use?
mcqs.explanation: Arrays store elements in contiguous (consecutive) memory locations, which enables efficient random access but requires a single large block of memory.

mcqs.question: In a 2D array arr[3][4], how many rows and columns are there?
mcqs.explanation: In arr[3][4], the first dimension (3) represents rows and the second dimension (4) represents columns. So there are 3 rows and 4 columns.

mcqs.question: What is the maximum number of elements a one-dimensional array of size n can store?
mcqs.explanation: An array of size n can store exactly n elements, indexed from 0 to n-1 in zero-based languages.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of inserting an element at position i in an array of n elements?
mcqs.explanation: Insertion requires shifting all elements from position i to n-1 one position right. In worst case (i=0), n elements are shifted, giving O(n).

mcqs.question: Which programming language uses column-major order for 2D arrays?
mcqs.explanation: Fortran and MATLAB use column-major order, where elements are stored column by column. C, C++, Java, and Python use row-major order.

mcqs.question: What is a jagged array?

options.text: A 2D array with equal rows and columns
A 2D array where each row can have different lengths

mcqs.explanation: A jagged array is an array of arrays where each inner array can have different lengths, like int[][] arr = {{1,2}, {3,4,5}, {6}};

mcqs.question: In row-major order, what is the formula to calculate address of element A[i][j] in array A[m][n]?
mcqs.explanation: In row-major order, elements of each row are stored consecutively. To reach A[i][j], we skip i complete rows (each of n elements) and j elements in the current row.

mcqs.question: What is the space complexity of storing an array of n integers?
mcqs.explanation: An array of n elements requires O(n) space, where n is the number of elements stored.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is NOT an advantage of arrays?
mcqs.explanation: Dynamic size adjustment is NOT an advantage of basic arrays. Static arrays have fixed size, while dynamic arrays (a different data structure) can resize but with overhead.

mcqs.question: If an array declared as int arr[10] starts at memory address 1000, what is the address of arr[5]? (Assume int is 4 bytes)
mcqs.explanation: Address = Base + (index × size) = 1000 + (5 × 4) = 1000 + 20 = 1020

mcqs.question: What is the worst-case time complexity for searching an element in an unsorted array?
mcqs.explanation: In an unsorted array, we must check every element in the worst case (element not present or at last position), giving O(n) time complexity.

mcqs.question: What happens when you insert into a full static array?
mcqs.explanation: A static array has fixed size. When it's full, attempting to insert a new element causes array overflow error or undefined behavior.

mcqs.question: Which data structure would you choose for frequent insertions at the beginning of a list?
mcqs.explanation: Linked lists provide O(1) insertion at any position because they only require pointer manipulation. Arrays require O(n) shifting of all elements.

mcqs.question: What is the time complexity of deleting an element from the end of a dynamic array (like ArrayList)?
mcqs.explanation: Deletion from the end requires no shifting of elements. Only the size/count needs to be decremented, which is O(1).

mcqs.question: What is amortized O(1) insertion in dynamic arrays?
mcqs.back: Occasional O(n) insertions (when resizing occurs) are averaged out over many O(1) insertions, giving amortized O(1) time.

options.text: Occasional O(n) cost averaged over many operations

mcqs.explanation: Dynamic arrays occasionally double their capacity (O(n) operation). When spread over many insertions, the cost averages to O(1) per operation.

mcqs.question: In a 3D array arr[2][3][4], how many total elements can be stored?
mcqs.explanation: Total elements = 2 × 3 × 4 = 24. Each dimension is multiplied to get total capacity.

mcqs.question: Why are arrays cache-friendly?

options.text: Contiguous memory enables spatial locality and prefetching

mcqs.explanation: Due to contiguous memory allocation, when one element is accessed, nearby elements are also loaded into cache (spatial locality), making subsequent accesses faster.

mcqs.question: Which of the following operations is most efficient in a linked list compared to arrays?
mcqs.explanation: Linked lists excel at insertion at the beginning (O(1)) because it only requires updating a pointer, while arrays require shifting O(n) elements.
